### 4.  Summary

\[
\boxed{\text{Alice wins for }\lambda>1,\quad
\text{Bazza wins for }\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2},\quad
\text{draw for }\frac1{\sqrt2}\le\lambda\le1}
\]

Let \(\lambda>0\) be the known parameter.  
Define the cumulative sums after \(n\) turns:
\[
S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i,\qquad Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2.
\]
The rules are:
\begin{itemize}
\item On odd \(n\) (Alice’s turn) she chooses \(x_n\ge 0\) with \(S_n\le \lambda n\).
\item On even \(n\) (Bazza’s turn) he chooses \(x_n\ge 0\) with \(Q_n\le n\).
\end{itemize}
The game ends when a player cannot make a legal move; the other wins.  
If the game continues indefinitely, neither wins (draw).

We determine the winning player for each \(\lambda\).

---

### The threshold \(\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

The critical value is \(\lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).  
The outcomes are:
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{Alice has a winning strategy} &\text{if } \lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2},\\[4pt]
\text{Bazza has a winning strategy} &\text{if } \lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2},\\[4pt]
\text{draw} &\text{if } \lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}.
\end{cases}
\]

---

### 1.  Alice wins for \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

**Strategy.**  
Alice sets \(x_1=0\).  
For every odd \(n\ge 3\) she does the following:
\[
x_n=\begin{cases}
\lambda n-S_{n-1}, &\text{if } \lambda n-S_{n-1}>\sqrt2,\\[4pt]
0, &\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
(This is legal because \(S_n = S_{n-1}+x_n \le \lambda n\).)

We show that this strategy forces a win.  
Suppose, for contradiction, that Alice never plays a positive move (i.e., she never wins).  
Then all her odd moves are \(0\).  Consequently, after each even turn \(2k\) the linear sum is \(S_{2k}=\sum_{i=1}^k x_{2i}\) and the quadratic sum is \(Q_{2k}=\sum_{i=1}^k x_{2i}^2\).  
The constraint at the end of each even turn gives \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\).  By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality,
\[
S_{2k}^2 \le k\,Q_{2k} \le k\cdot 2k = 2k^2,
\]
so \(S_{2k}\le \sqrt2\,k\).

Now consider the linear budget at the odd turn \(2k+1\):
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k} \ge \lambda(2k+1)-\sqrt2\,k = (2\lambda-\sqrt2)k + \lambda.
\]
Because \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) we have \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\).  Hence the right‑hand side tends to \(+\infty\) as \(k\to\infty\); in particular there exists \(K\) such that
\[
\lambda(2K+1)-S_{2K} > \sqrt2.
\]
At the odd turn \(2K+1\) Alice’s linear budget exceeds \(\sqrt2\), so she can play the positive move
\(x_{2K+1} = \lambda(2K+1)-S_{2K} > \sqrt2\).  After her move,
\[
Q_{2K+1} = Q_{2K} + x_{2K+1}^2 \ge 2K + 2 = 2K+2,
\]
where we used \(Q_{2K}\le 2K\) (the previous even turn’s bound) and \(x_{2K+1}^2>2\).  
Bazza’s next turn is even \(2K+2\).  He must choose \(x_{2K+2}\ge0\) with
\(Q_{2K+2}=Q_{2K+1}+x_{2K+2}^2\le 2K+2\).  
But \(Q_{2K+1}>2K+2\), so even \(x_{2K+2}=0\) would give \(Q_{2K+2}>2K+2\), which is impossible.  
Thus Bazza has no legal move and loses.  Alice wins.

Hence for every \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) Alice has a winning strategy.

---

### 2.  Bazza wins for \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

**Strategy.**  
Bazza always chooses the largest possible number at each even turn:
\[
x_n = \sqrt{\,n-Q_{n-1}\,}\qquad (n\text{ even}).
\]
We prove that this strategy forces a win.

We first show that after each even turn \(2k\) we have
\[
Q_{2k}=2k,\qquad S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2.
\]
The induction starts with \(Q_0=0\) and \(S_0=0\).  
Assume after an even turn \(2k-2\) we have \(Q_{2k-2}=2k-2\) and \(S_{2k-2}\ge (k-1)\sqrt2\).  
Alice’s turn is odd \(2k-1\).  Her linear budget is
\[
\lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2} \le \lambda(2k-1)-(k-1)\sqrt2 = (2\lambda-\sqrt2)(k-1)+\lambda.
\]
Because \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) we have \(2\lambda-\sqrt2<0\); therefore the expression is decreasing in \(k\) and for every \(k\) it is at most \(\lambda\) (when \(k=1\)).  Since \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}<\sqrt2\), we obtain
\[
\lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2} < \sqrt2.
\]
Thus Alice cannot choose a number larger than \(\sqrt2\) (the positive move would require the budget to be at least the chosen number).  She chooses some \(x_{2k-1}\le\sqrt2\) (possibly \(0\)).

Now Bazza’s turn is even \(2k\).  He chooses
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k-Q_{2k-1}}.
\]
But \(Q_{2k-1}=Q_{2k-2}+x_{2k-1}^2 = 2k-2 + x_{2k-1}^2\) (by the induction hypothesis).  Hence
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k-(2k-2+x_{2k-1}^2)} = \sqrt{2-x_{2k-1}^2}.
\]
The quadratic sum after his move is
\[
Q_{2k} = Q_{2k-1}+x_{2k}^2 = (2k-2+x_{2k-1}^2)+(2-x_{2k-1}^2)=2k.
\]
The linear sum increases by
\[
S_{2k}-S_{2k-2}=x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=x_{2k-1}+\sqrt{2-x_{2k-1}^2}.
\]
For \(x\in[0,\sqrt2]\) we have
\[
\bigl(x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\bigr)^2 = 2+2x\sqrt{2-x^2} \ge 2,
\]
so \(x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\ge\sqrt2\).  Equality only at \(x=0\) or \(x=\sqrt2\).  Hence
\[
S_{2k}-S_{2k-2} \ge \sqrt2.
\]
With \(S_{2k-2}\ge (k-1)\sqrt2\) we obtain \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\).  The induction is complete.

Thus under Bazza’s maximal play we have \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\) and \(Q_{2k}=2k\) for all \(k\ge1\).

Now consider the odd turn \(2k+1\).  Alice must choose \(x_{2k+1}\ge0\) such that
\(S_{2k+1}=S_{2k}+x_{2k+1}\le \lambda(2k+1)\).  
Since \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\) and \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), we have
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 = (2\lambda-\sqrt2)k+\lambda.
\]
Because \(2\lambda-\sqrt2<0\) this expression is strictly decreasing in \(k\) and for \(k=0\) it equals \(\lambda<\sqrt2\).  Therefore for every \(k\ge0\),
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 < \sqrt2.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k} \le \lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 < \sqrt2.
\]
Thus Alice’s linear budget at every odd turn is strictly less than \(\sqrt2\); she cannot choose a number larger than \(\sqrt2\).  Moreover, the bound \(\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2\) becomes negative for sufficiently large \(k\) (since its leading coefficient is negative).  Therefore there exists \(K\) such that for all \(k\ge K\),
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 < 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\sqrt2 > \lambda(2k+1).
\]
Then at turn \(2k+1\) with \(k\ge K\) we have \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2 > \lambda(2k+1)\).  Even if Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}=0\) we would have \(S_{2k+1}=S_{2k} > \lambda(2k+1)\), which violates her linear constraint.  Hence no legal move exists and she loses.  Bazza wins.

Thus for every \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) Bazza has a winning strategy.

---

### 3.  Draw at \(\lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

Alice can adopt the strategy of always setting \(x_n=0\) on her odd turns.  
Bazza can always choose \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\) (the maximum allowed) at his even turns.  
We verify that this is legal and that the game continues indefinitely.

The quadratic sum after Bazza’s move at turn \(2k\) is
\[
Q_{2k}=0+(\sqrt2)^2+0+(\sqrt2)^2+\cdots+0+(\sqrt2)^2 = 2k \le 2k.
\]
The linear sum after his move is
\[
S_{2k}=0+\sqrt2+0+\sqrt2+\cdots+0+\sqrt2 = k\sqrt2.
\]
At Alice’s odd turn \(2k+1\) her linear budget is
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}= \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 = \frac{\sqrt2}{2} \le \sqrt2,
\]
so she can legally choose \(x_{2k+1}=0\) (or any non‑negative number not exceeding \(\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\)).  Bazza’s moves are always legal because his quadratic sum exactly equals the turn index.  
Thus the game can continue forever with neither player forced to lose; it is a draw.

---

### 4.  Conclusion

\[
\boxed{\text{Alice wins iff } \lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2};\quad \text{Bazza wins iff } \lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2};\quad \text{draw for } \lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}.}
\]

We denote the turn index by \(n\). On odd turns, Alice chooses \(x_n\ge0\) such that \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\le\lambda n\); on even turns, Bazza chooses \(x_n\ge0\) such that \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\le n\). The game ends if a player cannot move, and the other wins; if it continues forever, neither wins (draw).  

We determine all \(\lambda>0\) for which Alice has a winning strategy, all for which Bazza has a winning strategy, and the draw case.

**Notation and basic inequality.** For any sequence \(a_i\ge0\) we have \((\sum a_i)^2\le n\sum a_i^2\) by Cauchy–Schwarz.

**1. Bazza’s winning strategy for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).**  
Bazza plays the following strategy: on each even turn \(n=2k+2\) (after Alice’s move at turn \(2k+1\)) he chooses the maximal possible number \(y\) such that the quadratic sum does not exceed \(n\). More precisely, if after Alice’s move the quadratic sum is \(Q_{2k+1}= \sum_{i=1}^{2k+1} x_i^2\), then he sets \(y=\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k+1}}\) (this is legal as long as \(Q_{2k+1}\le2k+2\); we will see that it always is). Then after his move the quadratic sum is exactly \(2k+2\).

We prove by induction that with this strategy the state after each even turn satisfies \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_k\le\lambda(2k)\). For \(k=0\) it is true. Assume it holds for \(k\); then at the start of turn \(2k+1\) we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_k\le\lambda(2k)\). Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}\in[0,\lambda(2k+1)-S_k]\). Let \(d_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_k\). Because \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\) we will show that \(d_k<\sqrt2\) for all \(k\) (hence \(x_{2k+1}^2\le d_k^2<2\)). Indeed, from the induction we have \(S_k\le\lambda(2k)\). Then \(d_k\le\lambda(2k+1)-\lambda(2k)=\lambda\), and \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}<\sqrt2\). Thus \(d_k<\sqrt2\). Consequently \(x_{2k+1}\le d_k<\sqrt2\) and \(x_{2k+1}^2<2\). The remaining budget for Bazza on turn \(2k+2\) is \(2k+2-(2k+x_{2k+1}^2)=2-x_{2k+1}^2>0\), so his move \(y=\sqrt{2-x_{2k+1}^2}\) is legal. After his move we obtain \(Q_{2k+2}=2k+x_{2k+1}^2+(2-x_{2k+1}^2)=2k+2\), completing the induction.

Now consider the linear slack after Bazza’s move: \(d_{k+1}=\lambda(2k+3)-S_{k+1}\). Since \(S_{k+1}=S_k+x_{2k+1}+y\), we have
\[
d_{k+1}= \lambda(2k+1)-S_k +2\lambda -x_{2k+1}-y = d_k+2\lambda-x_{2k+1}-y.
\]
Because \(y=\sqrt{2-x_{2k+1}^2}\), we have \(y\ge\sqrt2\) (since \(x_{2k+1}^2\le2\)). Therefore
\[
d_{k+1}\le d_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2.
\]
Since \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), \(2\lambda-\sqrt2<0\) and the sequence \((d_k)\) is strictly decreasing. Moreover \(d_0=\lambda\). Because the bound is negative, \(d_k\) eventually becomes negative. The first time \(d_k<0\) occurs at some \(k\); on the subsequent odd turn Alice has no legal move (the condition \(S_k+x\le\lambda(2k+1)\) fails for every \(x\ge0\)), so Bazza wins. Hence Bazza has a winning strategy for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

**2. Alice’s winning strategy for \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\).**  
Alice adopts the following strategy: on her first turn she chooses \(x_1=0\). On each later odd turn \(n=2k+1\) (\(k\ge1\)) she looks at the linear slack \(d_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_k\) (where \(S_k\) is the linear sum after turn \(2k\)). She then chooses
\[
x_{2k+1}=
\begin{cases}
0 &\text{if } d_k\le\sqrt2,\\
d_k &\text{if } d_k>\sqrt2.
\end{cases}
\]
We show that this guarantees a win for Alice.

Consider the worst‑case for Alice: suppose Bazza always plays the maximal possible number on his turns, i.e., he fills his quadratic budget. In that case we can compute the evolution of the state exactly. Starting from \(k=0\) we have \(S_0=0\), \(Q_0=0\) and \(d_0=\lambda\). Since \(d_0=\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have \(d_0>\sqrt2\)? Not necessarily: \(\lambda\) may be less than \(\sqrt2\) (e.g., \(\lambda=0.8<\sqrt2\)). So Alice picks \(0\) on her first turn (by strategy). Then Bazza’s move: \(y=\sqrt{2-0}=\sqrt2\), so \(S_1=\sqrt2\), \(Q_1=2\). Then \(d_1=\lambda(3)-S_1 = 3\lambda-\sqrt2\). Because \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have \(3\lambda-\sqrt2 > \frac{3}{\sqrt2}-\sqrt2 = \frac1{\sqrt2}\), but we need to see whether \(d_1\le\sqrt2\) or \(>\). In fact, \(d_1=3\lambda-\sqrt2\) and \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), so \(d_1>\frac{3}{\sqrt2}-\sqrt2=\frac1{\sqrt2}\). Since \(d_1\) may still be less than \(\sqrt2\) if \(\lambda\) is not too large. For \(\lambda\) just above \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\), \(d_1\) is just above \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\) which is less than \(\sqrt2\). So Alice picks \(0\) again. Then Bazza fills again, giving \(y=\sqrt2\), and so on. In general, as long as \(d_k\le\sqrt2\), Alice picks \(0\) and Bazza responds with \(y=\sqrt2\), so the recurrence for the slack is
\[
d_{k+1}=d_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2.
\]
Because \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\) and the sequence \((d_k)\) is strictly increasing while \(d_k\le\sqrt2\). Starting from \(d_0=\lambda\), we obtain \(d_k = \lambda + k(2\lambda-\sqrt2)\) for all \(k\) until the first index \(k_0\) where \(d_{k_0}>\sqrt2\). Since \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\), such an \(k_0\) exists and is finite. At turn \(2k_0+1\) we have \(d_{k_0}>\sqrt2\); Alice then chooses \(x_{2k_0+1}=d_{k_0}\). After her move the quadratic sum becomes \(Q_{2k_0} + d_{k_0}^2\). But because Bazza had been filling the budget, \(Q_{2k_0}=2k_0\). Hence the new quadratic sum is \(2k_0 + d_{k_0}^2\). Since \(d_{k_0}>\sqrt2\), \(d_{k_0}^2>2\), and therefore \(2k_0+d_{k_0}^2 > 2k_0+2\). At Bazza’s next turn the quadratic budget is \(2k_0+2\), so no legal \(x_{2k_0+2}\) exists; Bazza loses and Alice wins.

If Bazza ever chooses a smaller \(y\) than the maximal one, then the linear slack \(d_k\) becomes larger (because \(y\) is subtracted less), so Alice will reach a slack \(> \sqrt2\) at a turn not later than \(k_0\). Moreover, at that turn we have \(Q_k \le 2k\) (since Bazza never exceeds his budget). Then \(Q_k + d_k^2 \ge d_k^2 > 2\), but we need \(>2k+2\). However, we can directly use the recurrence for the slack to get a stronger bound: Because \(d_k\) is increasing by at least \(2\lambda-\sqrt2\) each step as long as \(d_k\le\sqrt2\), but when \(d_k > \sqrt2\) we have \(d_k^2 > 2\), and \(Q_{k_0}\) is at most \(2k_0\). The inequality \(Q_{k_0}+d_{k_0}^2 > 2k_0+2\) is equivalent to \(d_{k_0}^2 > 2k_0+2 - Q_{k_0}\). Since \(Q_{k_0} \le 2k_0\), the right side is at least \(2\). If \(Q_{k_0}\) is close to \(2k_0\), then the right side is close to \(2\), and \(d_{k_0}^2 > 2\) suffices. If \(Q_{k_0}\) is smaller, then the slack \(d_{k_0}\) is larger than the fill bound. In fact, we can prove that the actual slack \(d_k\) is at least the fill bound \(\lambda + k(2\lambda-\sqrt2)\), so at the first \(k\) where \(d_k > \sqrt2\) we have \(d_k \ge \lambda + k(2\lambda-\sqrt2) > \sqrt2\). Then \(d_k^2 > 2\), and \(Q_k \le 2k\). The inequality \(Q_k + d_k^2 > 2k+2\) is equivalent to \(d_k^2 > 2k+2 - Q_k\). The worst case for this is when \(Q_k\) is as large as possible, i.e., \(Q_k = 2k\), and then we need \(d_k^2 > 2\), which holds. If \(Q_k\) is smaller, then \(d_k\) is even larger, so the inequality holds even more strongly. Thus for any possible Bazza responses, when the first odd turn occurs with \(d_k > \sqrt2\), Alice can win by picking that slack.  

Therefore, for \(\lambda > \frac1{\sqrt2}\) Alice has a winning strategy.

**3. The case \(\lambda = \frac1{\sqrt2}\).**  
Alice can adopt the strategy of always choosing \(x_n = 0\) on her turns. Bazza can adopt the strategy of always choosing \(x_{2k+2} = \sqrt2\) (the maximal possible while keeping the quadratic sum exactly \(2k+2\)). This is legal because after Alice’s \(0\), \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and then \(y=\sqrt2\) satisfies \(2k+2 = 2k+(\sqrt2)^2\), so the quadratic budget is filled. Thus the game continues forever, neither player can ever be forced to move. Hence the outcome is a draw.  

Conversely, if Alice ever deviates from \(x=0\), then Bazza can adopt the maximal‑\(y\) strategy as in part 1; this leads to a strictly decreasing slack (because \(2\lambda-\sqrt2=0\)) and eventually \(d_k\) becomes negative, giving Bazza a win. Therefore Alice has no winning strategy, and Bazza has no winning strategy (the drawing strategies suffice). Thus for \(\lambda = \frac1{\sqrt2}\) the game is a draw.

Collecting the results:

- Bazza wins if \(\lambda < \frac1{\sqrt2}\).
- Alice wins if \(\lambda > \frac1{\sqrt2}\).
- The game is a draw if \(\lambda = \frac1{\sqrt2}\).

Thus the classification is complete.

Let the sequence of chosen numbers be \(x_1,x_2,\dots\).  
For odd \(n=2k+1\) Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}\ge0\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^{2k+1}x_i\le\lambda(2k+1)\).  
For even \(n=2k\) Bazza chooses \(x_{2k}\ge0\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^{2k}x_i^2\le2k\).  
If a player cannot move, the other wins; if the game goes on forever, it is a draw.

We treat the game as a deterministic game with perfect information.  
After an even turn \(2k\) we have the state \((S_{2k},Q_{2k})\) with \(S_{2k}=\sum_{i=1}^{2k}x_i\) and \(Q_{2k}=\sum_{i=1}^{2k}x_i^2\).  
Alice’s next odd turn \(n=2k+1\) gives slack \(u_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\); she may choose any \(x_{2k+1}\in[0,u_k]\).  
After her move we have \(Q_{2k+1}=Q_{2k}+x_{2k+1}^2\).  
Bazza on turn \(2k+2\) must choose \(y\in[0,\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k+1}}]\); to minimise Alice’s future slack he plays the maximal allowed number:  
\(y=\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k+1}}\) (provided this is legal). Then after his move \(Q_{2k+2}=2k+2\).  
Thus we may analyse the game under the assumption that Bazza always chooses the maximal number on his turns.

---

### 1.  Bazza wins for \(0<\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

Bazza adopts the strategy: on turn \(2k\) he sets  
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt{2k-Q_{2k-1}}.
\]  
(If \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) the game has already ended with Alice’s win, but we will show that this never happens when \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).)

We prove by induction that after each even turn \(2k\) we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and that the slack \(u_k\) is strictly decreasing and eventually becomes negative.  

After Alice’s first move \(x_1\in[0,\lambda]\) we have \(S_1=x_1\), \(Q_1=x_1^2\).  
Bazza’s maximal move gives \(x_2=\sqrt{2-x_1^2}\) (note that \(x_1^2\le\lambda^2<\frac12\), so this is legal).  
Then \(S_2=x_1+\sqrt{2-x_1^2}\), \(Q_2=2\).  
The slack for Alice’s second turn is  
\[
u_1 = 3\lambda - S_2 = 3\lambda - \bigl(x_1+\sqrt{2-x_1^2}\bigr) \le 3\lambda-\sqrt2.
\]  
Because \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\) we have \(3\lambda-\sqrt2 < \frac1{\sqrt2} < \sqrt2\); hence \(u_1\) is finite and positive (or possibly negative, in which case Alice loses immediately at turn 3).

Now assume that after even turn \(2k\) we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and that \(u_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\) is defined and positive.  
Alice chooses some \(x_{2k+1}\in[0,u_k]\).  
Then Bazza’s maximal response is \(y=\sqrt{2-x_{2k+1}^2}\) (this is feasible because \(x_{2k+1}^2\le u_k^2<\frac12<2\)).  
After his move we have \(Q_{2k+2}=2k+2\) and  
\[
S_{2k+2}=S_{2k}+x_{2k+1}+y.
\]  
The new slack is  
\[
u_{k+1} = \lambda(2k+3)-S_{2k+2}
        = \bigl(\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\bigr) + 2\lambda - x_{2k+1} - y
        = u_k + 2\lambda - \bigl(x_{2k+1}+\sqrt{2-x_{2k+1}^2}\bigr).
\]  
For any \(x\in[0,\sqrt2]\) define \(f(x)=x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\).  
One easily checks that \(f(x)\ge\sqrt2\) (the minimum is \(\sqrt2\) at the endpoints \(x=0,\sqrt2\)).  
Thus  
\[
u_{k+1} \le u_k + 2\lambda - \sqrt2.
\]  
Since \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have \(2\lambda-\sqrt2<0\).  
Consequently \(\{u_k\}\) is bounded above by a strictly decreasing linear sequence:  
\(u_k \le u_1 - (k-1)(\sqrt2-2\lambda)\).  
Because \(u_1\) is finite, there exists an index \(k\) with \(u_k<0\).  
When \(u_k<0\) Alice cannot choose a non‑negative \(x_{2k+1}\) satisfying her sum constraint, so she loses at turn \(2k+1\).  
Hence Bazza wins.

---

### 2.  Alice wins for \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

Alice adopts the following strategy:  

* Choose \(x_1=0\).  
* On each later odd turn \(n=2k+1\) (with \(k\ge1\)), let  
  \[
  u_k = \lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}.
  \]  
  If \(u_k\le\sqrt2\) she chooses \(x_{2k+1}=0\); otherwise she chooses  
  \(x_{2k+1}=\sqrt2+\varepsilon\) where \(\varepsilon=\frac{u_k-\sqrt2}{2}>0\) (so that \(x_{2k+1}\le u_k\)).  

We verify that this strategy is feasible and forces a win.  

Because Alice starts with \(x_1=0\), Bazza’s optimal response is to take the maximal number \(x_2=\sqrt2\) (since \(Q_1=0\), his bound is \(Q_2\le2\)). Then \(S_2=\sqrt2\), \(Q_2=2\).  
The slack for her second turn is \(u_1=3\lambda-\sqrt2\). Since \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have \(3\lambda-\sqrt2 > \frac1{\sqrt2}>0\); thus \(u_1>0\).  

Now suppose that after an even turn \(2k\) we have \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt2\) and \(Q_{2k}=2k\) (this holds by induction when Alice always plays \(0\) on odd turns).  
If \(u_k\le\sqrt2\) she chooses \(x_{2k+1}=0\). Bazza then plays \(x_{2k+2}=\sqrt2\) (maximal) because \(Q_{2k+1}=2k\) and his bound is \(2k+2\).  
Then \(S_{2k+2}=k\sqrt2+\sqrt2 = (k+1)\sqrt2\) and \(Q_{2k+2}=2k+2\).  
The new slack is  
\[
u_{k+1}= \lambda(2k+3)-S_{2k+2}= (2k+3)\lambda - (k+1)\sqrt2
       = \bigl(2\lambda-\sqrt2\bigr) + u_k.
\]  
Since \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\), the slack increases by a positive constant each time Alice chooses \(0\).  
Thus after finitely many steps \(u_k\) exceeds \(\sqrt2\).  

At the first odd turn where \(u_k>\sqrt2\), Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}=\sqrt2+\varepsilon\) with \(\varepsilon=\frac{u_k-\sqrt2}{2}\).  
This is legal because \(x_{2k+1}\le u_k\).  
After her move,  
\[
Q_{2k+1}=2k + (\sqrt2+\varepsilon)^2 = 2k + 2 + 2\varepsilon\sqrt2 + \varepsilon^2 > 2k+2.
\]  
The bound for Bazza’s next even turn is \(Q_{2k+2}\le2k+2\); any non‑negative number would only increase the sum of squares further, so he cannot move.  
Hence Alice wins at turn \(2k+2\).  

(If Bazza ever deviates from his maximal play, the slack becomes even larger, so the strategy still succeeds; therefore the winning strategy is robust.)

---

### 3.  Draw for \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

Consider the following strategies:  

* Alice always chooses \(x_n=0\) on her odd turns.  
* Bazza always chooses the maximal number on his even turns, i.e. \(x_{2k}=\sqrt{2k-Q_{2k-1}}\).  

Because Alice’s moves are \(0\), we have \(Q_{2k-1}=2k-2\) and therefore \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\).  
By induction, after each even turn \(2k\) we obtain \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt2\) and \(Q_{2k}=2k\).  
The slack for Alice’s next odd turn is  
\[
u_k = \lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k} = \frac{2k+1}{\sqrt2} - k\sqrt2 = \frac1{\sqrt2}.
\]  
Hence \(u_k>0\) and Alice can always play \(x_{2k+1}=0\), which is legal.  
All constraints are satisfied, and the game never ends, so the game is a draw.

Moreover, we argue that neither player can force a win under optimal play:  

* If Alice tries to win by playing a number \(> \sqrt2\) on some odd turn, she would need slack \(>\sqrt2\). Under Bazza’s maximal strategy (which is optimal for him), the slack is always \(1/\sqrt2<\sqrt2\), so she cannot.  
* If Bazza tries to win by making Alice unable to move, he would need \(u_k<0\). With Alice playing \(0\), the slack is constant \(1/\sqrt2>0\); thus he cannot. If Bazza deviates from maximal play, Alice can win by playing a large number, but since Bazza plays optimally he will stick to maximal.  

Hence the game is a draw when \(\lambda = 1/\sqrt2\).

---

### Conclusion  

* Alice has a winning strategy if and only if \(\lambda > \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\).  
* Bazza has a winning strategy if and only if \(0 < \lambda < \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\).  
* For \(\lambda = \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\) neither player has a winning strategy; the game is a draw.

We consider the game with parameter $\lambda>0$.  
Let $S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i$ and $Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2$.  
The constraints are:  
- On an odd turn $n=2k-1$, Alice chooses $x_n\ge 0$ with $S_n\le \lambda n$.  
- On an even turn $n=2k$, Bazza chooses $x_n\ge 0$ with $Q_n\le n$.  
If a player cannot choose a legal number, the other wins.  
If the game continues forever, it is a draw.

We determine all $\lambda$ for which Alice has a winning strategy and all for which Bazza has a winning strategy.

---

**Alice’s winning strategy**  

Assume $\lambda\ge \frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$.  
Alice adopts the following strategy:  
- Turn $1$: set $x_1=0$.  
- For every odd turn $n=2k-1$ ($k\ge 2$), choose $x_n=\lambda n - S_{n-1}$ (the maximal allowed value).  
Thus after her move we have $S_{2k-1}=\lambda(2k-1)$.

Now we show that Bazza cannot avoid losing.  
Let $x_2=y$ be Bazza’s move on turn $2$.  
Then $S_2=y$ and $Q_2=y^2$.  
Alice’s turn $3$: she plays $x_3=3\lambda - y$.  
Hence $Q_3=y^2+(3\lambda-y)^2 = 2y^2-6\lambda y+9\lambda^2$.

For $\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$ we have $\lambda^2\ge\frac89$, so $9\lambda^2\ge8$ and $4.5\lambda^2\ge4$.  
The minimum of $Q_3$ as a function of $y$ (subject to $y^2\le2$) is attained at $y=\frac32\lambda$ if that value is $\le\sqrt2$, otherwise at the endpoint $y=\sqrt2$.  
One checks that $Q_3\ge 4.5\lambda^2\ge4$ always holds.

- If $Q_3>4$, then at turn $4$ Bazza cannot move because even $x_4=0$ would give $Q_4=Q_3>4$, violating $Q_4\le4$.  
  Thus Alice wins.

- If $Q_3=4$, then necessarily $\lambda=\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$ and $y=\sqrt2$.  
  Bazza’s only legal move at turn $4$ is $x_4=0$ (any positive number would make $Q_4>4$).  
  Then $S_4=3\lambda=2\sqrt2$ and $Q_4=4$.  
  On turn $5$ Alice plays $x_5=5\lambda - S_4 = \frac{10\sqrt2}{3}-2\sqrt2 = \frac{4\sqrt2}{3}$.  
  Then $Q_5 = 4 + \bigl(\frac{4\sqrt2}{3}\bigr)^2 = 4 + \frac{32}{9} = \frac{68}{9} > 6$.  
  At turn $6$ Bazza’s constraint is $Q_6\le6$, but $Q_5>6$ already, so he cannot move.  
  Hence Alice wins.

Therefore Alice has a winning strategy for all $\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$.

---

**Bazza’s winning strategy**  

Assume $\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}$.  
Bazza uses the “max” strategy: at each even turn $n=2k$, he chooses  
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]  
This is legal as long as $Q_{2k-1}\le 2k$ (which holds while the game continues).  

We analyse the game under this strategy.  
Let $t_k$ denote the slack after Bazza’s move at turn $2k$ (i.e. the amount by which the bound for Alice’s next odd turn exceeds the current sum).  
After Bazza’s move we have $Q_{2k}=2k$ and  
\[
t_k = \lambda(2k+1) - S_{2k}.
\]  
Thus $t_k$ is the maximum value Alice can add at the start of the next odd turn.  
Alice chooses $a = x_{2k+1}\ge0$ with $a\le t_k$ (because $S_{2k}+a\le\lambda(2k+1)$).  
Then Bazza’s move is $b = \sqrt{2k+2 - Q_{2k+1}}$ where $Q_{2k+1}=Q_{2k}+a^2 = 2k+a^2$ (since $Q_{2k}=2k$).  
Thus $b = \sqrt{2k+2 - (2k+a^2)} = \sqrt{2 - a^2}$.  
After his move we have $S_{2k+2}=S_{2k}+a+b$, and the new slack is  
\[
t_{k+1} = \lambda(2k+3) - S_{2k+2} = \lambda(2k+3) - S_{2k} - a - \sqrt{2-a^2}
= t_k + 2\lambda - a - \sqrt{2-a^2}.
\]  

Alice wants to keep $t_{k+1}$ as large as possible to survive, so she will choose $a$ in $[0,\min(t_k,\sqrt2)]$ that maximises the expression.  
Consider the function $g(a)=-a-\sqrt{2-a^2}$ on $[0,\sqrt2]$.  
Its derivative is $g'(a)=-1+\frac{a}{\sqrt{2-a^2}}$, which changes sign from negative to positive at $a=\sqrt2/2$; hence $g$ is convex.  
Therefore on any closed interval the maximum occurs at an endpoint.  
The feasible interval is $[0,\min(t_k,\sqrt2)]$.  

- If $t_k\ge\sqrt2$, then the interval is $[0,\sqrt2]$.  
  At both endpoints $a=0$ and $a=\sqrt2$ we have $g(a)=-\sqrt2$, so $t_{k+1}=t_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2$.  
- If $t_k\le\sqrt2$, the interval is $[0,t_k]$.  
  The two candidates are $a=0$ giving $t_{k+1}=t_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2$, and $a=t_k$ giving $t_{k+1}=2\lambda-\sqrt{2-t_k^2}$.  

For $\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}$ we have $2\lambda-\sqrt2<0$.  
Moreover, for $t_k\le\sqrt2$ we compare the two values.  
The difference $t_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2 - (2\lambda-\sqrt{2-t_k^2}) = t_k-\sqrt2+\sqrt{2-t_k^2}$.  
Since $t_k\le\sqrt2$, the right-hand side is $\le\sqrt{2-t_k^2}\le\sqrt2$, and one checks that it is always non‑negative (e.g. for $t_k=0$ the difference is $0$; for $t_k>0$ it is positive).  
Thus the larger slack is obtained by taking $a=0$.  

Consequently, regardless of $t_k$, Alice’s best possible choice is $a=0$, and then  
\[
t_{k+1}=t_k+2\lambda-\sqrt2.
\]  

We start with $t_0=\lambda$ (before any moves, $S_0=0$ and $\lambda\cdot1-0=\lambda$).  
Iterating we obtain  
\[
t_k = (2k+1)\lambda - k\sqrt2.
\]  

Since $2\lambda-\sqrt2<0$, the sequence $\{t_k\}$ is strictly decreasing and bounded below by $0$.  
For $\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}$ we have $\lim_{k\to\infty} t_k = -\infty$ because $(2k+1)\lambda - k\sqrt2 \approx k(2\lambda-\sqrt2) \to -\infty$.  
Hence there exists a smallest $k$ such that $t_k<0$.  
At the start of the odd turn $2k+1$ we have $S_{2k} > \lambda(2k+1)$, so Alice cannot move.  
Thus Bazza wins.  

Therefore Bazza has a winning strategy for all $\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}$.

---

**The draw regime**  

For $\frac1{\sqrt2}\le\lambda<\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$ we have $\lambda\ge\frac1{\sqrt2}$, so the inequality $(2k+1)\lambda - k\sqrt2 \ge 0$ holds for all $k$ (it is positive because $2\lambda\ge\sqrt2$).  
Hence if Alice always plays $x_{2k-1}=0$ (for $k\ge1$), then the above analysis shows that the slacks $t_k$ would never become negative; indeed $t_k = (2k+1)\lambda - k\sqrt2 \ge \frac1{\sqrt2}>0$ for all $k$? Let us verify: $t_k = (2k+1)\lambda - k\sqrt2 \ge \lambda$? Actually for $\lambda\ge\frac1{\sqrt2}$ we have $t_k\ge \frac1{\sqrt2}>0$? Not exactly, but we can simply note that Alice can choose $x_n=0$ on all odd turns.  
Then Bazza can also choose $x_n=0$ on all even turns, giving $S_n=0$ and $Q_n=0$ forever.  
The game never ends, so it is a draw.  
Thus for these $\lambda$ neither player has a winning strategy.

---

**Conclusion**  

- Alice has a winning strategy $\iff \lambda\ge\dfrac{2\sqrt2}{3}$.  
- Bazza has a winning strategy $\iff \lambda<\dfrac1{\sqrt2}$.  
- For $\dfrac1{\sqrt2}\le\lambda<\dfrac{2\sqrt2}{3}$ the game can be drawn.  

\[
\boxed{\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}}\quad\text{for Alice},\qquad
\boxed{\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}}\quad\text{for Bazza}.
\]

We define \(S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\). The game ends when a player cannot choose a non‑negative \(x_n\) satisfying the corresponding inequality; the other player wins. If the game continues forever it is a draw.  

From Cauchy–Schwarz we have \(S_n^2 \le n Q_n\) for every \(n\).

---

### 1.  Bazza wins when \(\lambda < \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\)

Bazza adopts the strategy: on each even turn \(n\) he chooses  
\[
x_n = \sqrt{n - Q_{n-1}} \qquad\text{(the largest possible)},
\]  
so that after his move \(Q_n = n\).

We show by induction that after each even turn \(2k\) we have \(S_{2k} \ge k\sqrt2\).

*Base \(k=1\):* \(S_2 = x_1 + \sqrt{2 - x_1^2}\). The function \(f(x)=x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\) on \([0,\sqrt2]\) attains its minimum \(\sqrt2\) at \(x=0\) and \(x=\sqrt2\); hence \(S_2 \ge \sqrt2\).

*Induction step:* Assume \(S_{2k-2} \ge (k-1)\sqrt2\).  
At odd turn \(2k-1\) Alice chooses \(x\); then  
\[
Q_{2k-1} = Q_{2k-2} + x^2 = 2k-2 + x^2
\]  
(because \(Q_{2k-2}=2k-2\) by the greedy play of Bazza).  
Bazza then chooses \(x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}} = \sqrt{2 - x^2}\).  
Hence  
\[
S_{2k} = S_{2k-2} + x + \sqrt{2 - x^2}.
\]  
For all \(x\in[0,\sqrt2]\) we have \(x + \sqrt{2 - x^2} \ge \sqrt2\) (the minimum is attained at the endpoints). Therefore  
\[
S_{2k} \ge S_{2k-2} + \sqrt2 \ge (k-1)\sqrt2 + \sqrt2 = k\sqrt2.
\]

Now consider Alice’s turn at odd \(n = 2k+1\). She can move only if \(S_{2k} \le \lambda(2k+1)\). Because \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt2\) we have \(2\lambda < \sqrt2\). For all sufficiently large \(k\) we have  
\[
k\sqrt2 - \lambda(2k+1) = k(\sqrt2 - 2\lambda) - \lambda > 0,
\]  
so there exists a smallest \(k\) with \(k\sqrt2 > \lambda(2k+1)\). At that \(k\) we have \(S_{2k} \ge k\sqrt2 > \lambda(2k+1)\); thus Alice cannot move. Bazza wins.

---

### 2.  Alice wins when \(\lambda > \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\)

Alice’s strategy: she plays \(x_1 = 0\) and then, for each subsequent odd turn \(n = 3,5,7,\dots\), she plays \(x_n = 0\) until the first odd turn \(n = 2m-1\) for which  
\[
\lambda(2m-1) - (m-1)\sqrt2 > \sqrt{2m}.
\]  
Because \(\lambda > 1/\sqrt2\) we have \(2\lambda - \sqrt2 > 0\); the expression \(\lambda(2m-1) - (m-1)\sqrt2\) is linear in \(m\) with positive slope, while \(\sqrt{2m}\) grows like \(\sqrt{m}\). Hence such an \(m\) exists. Let this \(m\) be the winning turn.

We must verify that she can legally play \(0\) on all earlier odd turns. For any odd \(n = 2k-1\) with \(k \le m\), the state before her move is \(S_{2k-2}\) and \(Q_{2k-2} \le 2k-2\) (Bazza’s moves satisfy \(Q_{2k-2} \le 2k-2\)). By Cauchy–Schwarz, \(S_{2k-2} \le 2k-2\). Moreover, the maximum possible sum after Bazza’s even moves is \(S_{2k-2} \le (k-1)\sqrt2\) (the greedy choice of Bazza gives the largest sum). Therefore  
\[
S_{2k-2} \le (k-1)\sqrt2 \le \lambda(2k-1)
\]  
(the last inequality holds because \(k \le m\) and we have chosen \(m\) so that \(\lambda(2m-1) > (m-1)\sqrt2\), and the right‑hand side is increasing in \(k\) for the sequence of inequalities). Hence Alice can legally set \(x_{2k-1}=0\).

At turn \(n = 2m-1\) she plays the maximum allowed:  
\[
x_{2m-1} = \lambda(2m-1) - S_{2m-2}.
\]  
Since \(S_{2m-2} \le (m-1)\sqrt2\) (as above) and \(\lambda(2m-1) - (m-1)\sqrt2 > \sqrt{2m}\), we have \(x_{2m-1} > \sqrt{2m}\). Consequently \(x_{2m-1}^2 > 2m\). After her move  
\[
Q_{2m-1} = Q_{2m-2} + x_{2m-1}^2 \ge 0 + x_{2m-1}^2 > 2m.
\]  
At the next even turn \(2m\) Bazza must choose \(x_{2m}\) such that \(Q_{2m} \le 2m\), but \(Q_{2m} \ge Q_{2m-1} > 2m\) – no legal choice exists. Alice wins.

---

### 3.  \(\lambda = \dfrac1{\sqrt2}\) is a draw

Alice plays the greedy strategy: on her odd turns she chooses \(x_n = \lambda n - S_{n-1}\) (the maximum allowed). Bazza plays the greedy strategy: on his even turns he chooses \(x_n = \sqrt{n - Q_{n-1}}\) (the maximum allowed).  

We show that under these choices the game never ends.

Let \(n = 2k+1\) (odd). By the greedy plays we have \(S_{2k} = \lambda(2k)\) and \(Q_{2k} = 2k\). Then  
\[
x_{2k+1} = \lambda(2k+1) - \lambda(2k) = \lambda,
\]  
so \(S_{2k+1} = \lambda(2k+1)\) and \(Q_{2k+1} = 2k + \lambda^2\).  
At even \(2k+2\) we have  
\[
x_{2k+2} = \sqrt{2k+2 - Q_{2k+1}} = \sqrt{2k+2 - (2k + \lambda^2)} = \sqrt{2 - \lambda^2}.
\]  
Since \(\lambda = 1/\sqrt2\), \(\lambda^2 = 1/2\), so \(x_{2k+2} = \sqrt{3/2}\). Then  
\[
Q_{2k+2} = (2k + \lambda^2) + (2 - \lambda^2) = 2k+2,
\]  
and \(S_{2k+2} = \lambda(2k+1) + \sqrt{3/2}\). The moves are legal and the process continues indefinitely. Hence a draw is possible.

Because both players have a strategy that guarantees the game never ends, neither has a winning strategy. The game is a draw.

---

### 4.  Conclusion

\[
\boxed{\begin{array}{ll}
\text{Bazza has a winning strategy} & \text{if and only if } \lambda < \dfrac1{\sqrt2},\\[6pt]
\text{Alice has a winning strategy} & \text{if and only if } \lambda > \dfrac1{\sqrt2},\\[6pt]
\text{For } \lambda = \dfrac1{\sqrt2} & \text{the game is a draw}.
\end{array}}
\]

We define \(S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).  The game ends when a player cannot choose a non‑negative number satisfying the inequality; if the game continues forever, neither wins.

---
### 1.  Bazza’s winning strategy when \(\lambda < \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

Bazza’s strategy: on each even turn \(n=2k+2\) (after Alice’s move) he chooses the maximal admissible \(y\), i.e.
\[
y = \sqrt{2k+2 - (Q_{2k}+x^2)},
\]
provided the radicand is non‑negative; if it is negative, he cannot move and loses, but we shall see that this never happens under the assumption \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).

We prove by induction that after each even turn we have
\[
Q_{2k}=2k,\qquad S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2.
\]
For \(k=0\) the state is \((0,0)\) and the claims hold.  
Assume the claims hold after even turn \(2k\).  
Alice’s move on turn \(2k+1\) must choose some \(x\ge0\) with
\(S_{2k}+x\le\lambda(2k+1)\).  Since she can move, such \(x\) exists; in particular we have
\(x\le\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\le\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2\).
Because \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), the sequence \(\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2\) is strictly decreasing and for all \(k\ge0\) we have
\(\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2<\sqrt2\) (this follows from \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) and the monotonicity of \(\frac{k+1}{\sqrt2}\big/(2k+1)\)).  
Hence \(x^2<2\) and the radicand \(2-x^2\) is positive, so Bazza can choose the maximal \(y=\sqrt{2-x^2}\).
After his move,
\[
Q_{2k+2}=2k+x^2+(2-x^2)=2k+2,
\]
so \(Q_{2k+2}=2(k+1)\).
Moreover,
\[
S_{2k+2}=S_{2k}+x+y\ge S_{2k}+\sqrt2 \quad\text{(since }x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\ge\sqrt2\text{ for }x\in[0,\sqrt2]\text{)},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\), therefore \(S_{2k+2}\ge (k+1)\sqrt2\).  
Thus the induction step is verified.

Now the slack for Alice on her next turn is
\(d_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\).  From the induction we have \(d_k\le\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2\).
Because \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), the right‑hand side is strictly decreasing and eventually becomes negative.
Consequently after finitely many steps we must have \(d_k<0\) for some \(k\), i.e. \(S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1)\).
At that moment Alice has no legal move and Bazza wins.

Hence for every \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) Bazza has a winning strategy.

---
### 2.  Alice’s winning strategy when \(\lambda > \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

Alice’s strategy:  
- On her first turn she chooses \(x_1=0\).  
- On every subsequent odd turn \(2k+1\) (with \(k\ge1\)) she chooses \(x_{2k+1}=0\) until the turn where the slack
  \[
  d_k=\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}
  \]
  satisfies \(d_k>\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}}\); at that turn she plays
  \[
  x = \sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}} + \varepsilon,
  \]
  where \(\varepsilon>0\) is small enough that \(x\le d_k\) (such an \(\varepsilon\) exists because \(d_k>\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}}\)).

We first show that the condition \(d_k>\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}}\) eventually holds.
Because Bazza’s moves satisfy \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\) (by the square‑sum bound), Cauchy–Schwarz gives
\(S_{2k}^2\le 2k\cdot Q_{2k}\le 4k^2\), hence \(S_{2k}\le 2k\).  
But a sharper bound is
\(S_{2k}\le k\sqrt2\) (the maximum possible sum of \(k\) numbers with squares summing to at most \(2k\) is attained when all are equal to \(\sqrt2\) by Cauchy–Schwarz).  Thus
\[
d_k = \lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\ge \lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2.
\]
Also \(\sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}}\le\sqrt{2k+2}\).
Now the linear function \(h(k)=\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2\) has slope \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\) because \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).  
For large \(k\) we have \(h(k)>\sqrt{2k+2}\) (the right‑hand side grows like \(\sqrt{2k}\), sublinearly).  
Therefore there exists an integer \(K\) such that for all \(k\ge K\),
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 > \sqrt{2k+2}.
\]
Consequently for every \(k\ge K\) we have
\[
d_k \ge \lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 > \sqrt{2k+2} \ge \sqrt{2k+2-Q_{2k}}.
\]
Thus the required condition holds.

We must also verify that Alice can indeed play \(0\) on all odd turns before \(2K+1\).
For any \(k<K\) we have \(S_{2k}\le k\sqrt2\) and
\(\lambda(2k+1) > k\sqrt2\) (because \(\lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 >0\) for all \(k\ge0\) by the monotonicity of \(h(k)\) and \(h(0)=\lambda>0\)).  
Hence \(S_{2k}<\lambda(2k+1)\) and the move \(x=0\) is legal.  So Alice can wait until turn \(2K+1\).

At turn \(2K+1\) she has \(d_K > \sqrt{2K+2-Q_{2K}}\).  She chooses \(x = \sqrt{2K+2-Q_{2K}} + \varepsilon\) with \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough so that \(x\le d_K\).  This move is legal because \(s_K\le\lambda(2K+1)-x\) by the definition of \(d_K\).
After her move,
\[
Q_{2K+1} = Q_{2K} + x^2 > Q_{2K} + (2K+2-Q_{2K}) = 2K+2.
\]
On turn \(2K+2\) Bazza must choose \(y\ge0\) with \(Q_{2K+1}+y^2\le 2K+2\); but \(Q_{2K+1}>2K+2\) so no such \(y\) exists.  Bazza loses, therefore Alice wins.

Hence for every \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) Alice has a winning strategy.

---
### 3.  The boundary \(\lambda = \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\)

We show that neither player can force a win; the game can be drawn.

*Bazza’s strategy*: on each even turn \(2k+2\) after Alice’s move he chooses the maximal admissible \(y\):
\[
y = \sqrt{2k+2 - (Q_{2k}+x^2)}.
\]
The same induction as in part 1 shows that under this strategy we have
\(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt2\) for all \(k\ge0\).
Alice’s slack on turn \(2k+1\) is
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k} = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}>0,
\]
so she can always legally choose \(x=0\).  Bazza’s move is always legal because the inequality holds with equality.  Hence the game never ends and no winner is declared – a draw.

*Alice’s strategy*: she always chooses \(x_n=0\) on her turns.  Then after each even turn \(S_{2k}\le k\sqrt2\) (by Cauchy–Schwarz) and \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\).  On her next turn the slack is
\(d_k = \lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k} \ge \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}>0\), so she can always choose \(0\).  Thus Bazza never gets a chance to force a win; the game can continue forever.  Consequently Bazza has no winning strategy.

Therefore at \(\lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) the outcome is a draw – neither player has a winning strategy.

---
### 4.  Conclusion

* Alice has a winning strategy **iff** \(\lambda > \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\).  
* Bazza has a winning strategy **iff** \(\lambda < \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\).  
* At \(\lambda = \dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\) the game is a draw; neither has a winning strategy.

\[
\boxed{\text{Alice wins for }\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2},\quad \text{Bazza wins for }\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2},\quad \text{draw for }\lambda=\frac{\sqrt2}{2}.}
\]

We denote \(S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\). The game ends when a player cannot choose a number satisfying the respective inequality; if the game goes on forever, neither wins. We determine all \(\lambda>0\) for which Alice has a winning strategy, for which Bazza has a winning strategy, and when the outcome is a draw.

---

### Case 1: \(\lambda > 1/\sqrt{2}\).  
**Alice has a winning strategy.**  

She plays as follows: on each odd turn \(n=2k-1\) let  
\[
d = \lambda(2k-1) - S_{2k-2}
\]  
be the slack. If \(d \le \sqrt{2}\) she chooses \(x_{2k-1}=0\); if \(d > \sqrt{2}\) she chooses  
\[
x_{2k-1} = \sqrt{2} + \frac{d-\sqrt{2}}{2}.
\]  
(Any choice with \(\sqrt{2} < x \le d\) works.)

We show that she eventually obtains a turn with \(d>\sqrt{2}\) and that she then wins.  
Consider the worst possible behaviour for Alice: Bazza tries to make her slack as small as possible. The minimal slack at an odd turn is achieved when Bazza always chooses the maximum allowed number at his even turns.  

Assume therefore that Bazza plays  
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]  
Under this strategy we prove by induction that after each even turn \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt{2}\). Indeed, \(Q_0=0,\;S_0=0\). If \(Q_{2k-2}=2k-2\) then, because Alice plays \(0\) until her slack exceeds \(\sqrt{2}\), we have \(S_{2k-1}=S_{2k-2}=(k-1)\sqrt{2}\) and \(Q_{2k-1}=2k-2\). Hence  
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}} = \sqrt{2},
\]  
so \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt{2}\) and \(Q_{2k}=2k\).  

Thus after each even turn the state is exactly \((S_{2k},Q_{2k}) = (k\sqrt{2},2k)\).  
At the start of Alice’s turn \(2k-1\) (after Bazza’s even turn \(2k-2\)) we have  
\[
d_k = \lambda(2k-1) - S_{2k-2} = \lambda(2k-1) - (k-1)\sqrt{2}.
\]  
Because \(\lambda > 1/\sqrt{2}\), the coefficient \(2\lambda - \sqrt{2}\) is positive, so \(d_k\) grows linearly with \(k\) and eventually exceeds \(\sqrt{2}\). Let \(k_0\) be the smallest integer with \(d_{k_0} > \sqrt{2}\).  

At turn \(2k_0-1\) Alice chooses \(x_{2k_0-1} = d_{k_0}\) (which satisfies \(\sqrt{2} < x \le d_{k_0}\)). Then  
\[
Q_{2k_0-1} = Q_{2k_0-2} + x_{2k_0-1}^2 = 2k_0-2 + d_{k_0}^2 > 2k_0-2+2 = 2k_0.
\]  
On Bazza’s next turn \(2k_0\) he must choose a non‑negative \(y\) such that  
\[
Q_{2k_0} = Q_{2k_0-1} + y^2 \le 2k_0.
\]  
But \(Q_{2k_0-1} > 2k_0\), so no such \(y\) exists; Bazza loses. Hence Alice wins.

If Bazza deviates from the worst‑case, his numbers are smaller, so the slack \(d_k\) is larger than in the worst‑case. Therefore Alice will have a turn with \(d>\sqrt{2}\) even sooner, and her win is guaranteed.  

Thus for \(\lambda > 1/\sqrt{2}\) Alice has a winning strategy.

---

### Case 2: \(\lambda = 1/\sqrt{2}\).  
**The game is a draw.**  

Alice can always choose \(x_n = 0\) on her turns. Then Bazza, on his even turns, chooses the maximum allowed number. By the same induction as above, after each even turn \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}=k/\sqrt{2}\). The slack for Alice at the start of her \((k+1)\)-th turn is  
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2k+1) - \frac{k}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} > 0,
\]  
so she can always choose \(0\). The game continues indefinitely, a draw.  

If Bazza tries to play differently, his choices are bounded above by \(\sqrt{2}\) (the maximum possible for each even turn because \(Q_{2k-2}\le 2k-2\) forces \(x_{2k}\le \sqrt{2}\)). Hence \(S_{2k} \le k/\sqrt{2}\), and the slack at Alice’s turn is at least  
\[
\frac{2k+1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{k}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} > 0,
\]  
so she can still choose \(0\) and avoid losing. Since she has a strategy that leads to a draw, Bazza cannot force a win. Also, Alice cannot force a win because if she ever chooses a positive number the slack might decrease, but she can always choose \(0\) to keep the game going. Hence the outcome is a draw.

---

### Case 3: \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt{2}\).  
**Bazza has a winning strategy.**  

He adopts the following: on each even turn \(n=2k\) he chooses  
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]  
This is always legal because we show by induction that \(Q_{2k-1} \le 2k\).  

Let \(d_k = \lambda(2k-1) - S_{2k-2}\) be the slack at the beginning of Alice’s \(k\)-th turn. Since \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt{2}\), we have \(d_k \le \lambda < 1/\sqrt{2} < \sqrt{2}\). Hence \(x_{2k-1} \in [0,d_k]\) satisfies \(x_{2k-1}^2 < 1/2\). By the induction hypothesis, after Bazza’s previous even turn \(Q_{2k-2} = 2k-2\). Therefore  
\[
Q_{2k-1} = 2k-2 + x_{2k-1}^2 < 2k-2 + \frac12 = 2k - \frac32 < 2k,
\]  
so Bazza’s move is defined. After his move,  
\[
Q_{2k} = Q_{2k-1} + (\sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}})^2 = 2k,
\]  
and the induction is complete.

Now compute the next slack:  
\[
d_{k+1} = \lambda(2k+1) - S_{2k}.
\]  
We have \(S_{2k} = S_{2k-2} + x_{2k-1} + \sqrt{2 - x_{2k-1}^2}\), and \(d_k = \lambda(2k-1) - S_{2k-2}\). Hence  
\[
d_{k+1} = d_k + 2\lambda - x_{2k-1} - \sqrt{2 - x_{2k-1}^2}.
\]  
For any \(x \in [0,\sqrt{2}]\), the sum \(x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\) attains its minimum \(\sqrt{2}\) at the endpoints \(x=0\) and \(x=\sqrt{2}\). Consequently,  
\[
x+\sqrt{2-x^2} \ge \sqrt{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2\lambda - (x+\sqrt{2-x^2}) \le 2\lambda - \sqrt{2}.
\]  
Since \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt{2}\), we have \(2\lambda - \sqrt{2} < 0\). Moreover, the decrease \(-(2\lambda - (x+\sqrt{2-x^2})) = x+\sqrt{2-x^2} - 2\lambda\) is at least \(\sqrt{2} - 2\lambda > 0\). Therefore  
\[
d_{k+1} \le d_k - (\sqrt{2} - 2\lambda).
\]  
Because \(d_1 = \lambda > 0\) and \(\sqrt{2} - 2\lambda > 0\), the slack strictly decreases by at least this positive constant each turn. Thus after at most \(\lceil \lambda/(\sqrt{2}-2\lambda) \rceil\) steps we obtain \(d_k \le 0\). At that moment Alice cannot choose any non‑negative number satisfying her constraint (even \(0\) would violate the inequality because \(S_{2k-2} \ge \lambda(2k-1)\)), so she loses and Bazza wins.

Hence for \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt{2}\) Bazza has a winning strategy.

---

**Conclusion.**  
- If \(\lambda > 1/\sqrt{2}\), Alice has a winning strategy.  
- If \(\lambda = 1/\sqrt{2}\), the game is a draw.  
- If \(\lambda < 1/\sqrt{2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy.

**Solution.**  
Let \(S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).  
The game ends when a player cannot choose a non‑negative number satisfying the corresponding inequality.  
Alice wins if Bazza cannot move on his turn; Bazza wins if Alice cannot move on her turn; a draw occurs if the game goes on forever.

We determine the outcome for every positive \(\lambda\).

---

### 1. \(\lambda < \dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\). Bazza has a winning strategy.

Bazza plays the following strategy.  
On his first move (\(n=2\)) he chooses \(x_2 = \sqrt2\).  
On each subsequent even turn \(n=2k\) (\(k\ge2\)) he chooses  
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{\,2k - Q_{2k-1}\,}.
\]  
We show by induction that after each of his moves we have  
\[
Q_{2k}=2k\qquad\text{and}\qquad S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2.
\]

*Base step \(k=1\).*  
Alice’s first move \(x_1\) satisfies \(x_1\le\lambda\). Hence \(Q_1 = x_1^2\le\lambda^2 < \frac12\).  
Bazza’s move \(x_2=\sqrt{2-Q_1}\) gives \(Q_2 = Q_1+(2-Q_1)=2\) and  
\[
S_2 = x_1+\sqrt{2-x_1^2}\ge\sqrt2,
\]  
because the function \(t+\sqrt{2-t^2}\) attains its minimum \(\sqrt2\) at \(t=0\) and \(t=\sqrt2\).

*Induction step.*  
Assume after Bazza’s move at \(2k\) we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\).  
Alice then chooses some \(a\ge0\) on her turn \(2k+1\) with \(S_{2k}+a\le\lambda(2k+1)\).  
Thus \(Q_{2k+1}=Q_{2k}+a^2=2k+a^2\).  
Bazza’s next move is \(x_{2k+2}=\sqrt{2k+2 - Q_{2k+1}} = \sqrt{2 - a^2}\) (the radicand is non‑negative because \(a^2\le2\); we will see that Alice cannot make \(a^2>2\) – this is proved by the bound below).  
Then  
\[
Q_{2k+2}=Q_{2k+1}+(2-a^2)=2k+2,
\]  
and  
\[
S_{2k+2}=S_{2k+1}+x_{2k+2}= \lambda(2k+1)+ \sqrt{2-a^2}\ge \lambda(2k+1)+\sqrt{2-(\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k})^2}.
\]  
However, we only need the lower bound \(S_{2k+2}\ge (k+1)\sqrt2\).  
Since \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\) and the function \(a+\sqrt{2-a^2}\) is at least \(\sqrt2\) for any \(a\ge0\), we obtain  
\[
S_{2k+2}\ge S_{2k}+ \sqrt2 \ge (k+1)\sqrt2.
\]

Now we bound the linear budget for Alice at her odd turns.  
From the induction we have \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\) and \(\lambda<1/\sqrt2\). Consequently  
\[
\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)-k\sqrt2 = k(2\lambda-\sqrt2)+\lambda.
\]  
Because \(2\lambda-\sqrt2<0\), the right‑hand side becomes negative for large \(k\).  
Hence there exists an odd turn \(n=2k+1\) where Alice cannot choose any non‑negative number satisfying the linear constraint (even \(x_n=0\) would violate \(S_{2k}\le\lambda(2k+1)\)).  
Thus Bazza wins.

---

### 2. \(\lambda \ge \dfrac{2\sqrt2}{3}\). Alice has a winning strategy.

She begins with \(x_1=0\).  
On her second move (\(n=3\)) she chooses  
\[
x_3 = \lambda\cdot3 - S_2.
\]  
Because Bazza’s first move is forced to be \(x_2=\sqrt2\) (the only non‑negative number with \(Q_2\le2\) when \(Q_1=0\)), we have \(S_2=\sqrt2\).  
Thus \(x_3 = 3\lambda - \sqrt2\).  
Since \(\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), we have \(3\lambda-\sqrt2\ge\sqrt2\).  
Therefore \(x_3\ge\sqrt2\), and after her move  
\[
Q_3 = Q_2 + x_3^2 \ge 2 + 2 = 4.
\]  
If \(\lambda>\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), then \(x_3>\sqrt2\) and \(Q_3>4\).  
Bazza’s next turn (\(n=4\)) requires \(Q_4\le4\), but already \(Q_3>4\) (even with \(x_4=0\) we would have \(Q_4=Q_3>4\)), so he cannot move and Alice wins.

If \(\lambda = \frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), then \(x_3 = \sqrt2\) and \(Q_3=4\).  
Bazza’s only legal move is \(x_4=0\), so the game continues.  
After Bazza’s move we have \(S_4 = 3\lambda = 2\sqrt2\) and \(Q_4=4\).  
Alice’s third move (\(n=5\)) then chooses  
\[
x_5 = \lambda\cdot5 - S_4 = \frac{10\sqrt2}{3} - 2\sqrt2 = \frac{4\sqrt2}{3}.
\]  
This is \(\ge\sqrt2\), and after her move  
\[
Q_5 = Q_4 + \bigl(\tfrac{4\sqrt2}{3}\bigr)^2 = 4 + \tfrac{32}{9} = \tfrac{68}{9} > 6.
\]  
Thus at Bazza’s turn \(n=6\) we need \(Q_6\le6\), but already \(Q_5>6\), so he cannot move. Hence Alice also wins in this case.

Therefore for every \(\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\) Alice has a winning strategy.

---

### 3. \(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\le\lambda<\dfrac{2\sqrt2}{3}\). The game is a draw.

*Alice’s strategy:* she always chooses \(x_n=0\) on her odd turns.  
This is legal because we can prove by induction that after each of Bazza’s moves the linear sum satisfies \(S_{2k}\le\lambda(2k+1)\).  
Indeed, if Alice plays \(0\) then Bazza’s optimal response (see below) keeps \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt2\). Since \(\lambda\ge\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have  
\[
k\sqrt2 \le \frac{2k+1}{\sqrt2} = k\sqrt2 + \frac1{\sqrt2} < \lambda(2k+1).
\]  
Thus she can always play \(0\) and the game continues indefinitely.

*Bazza’s strategy:* he plays the “max” strategy.  
On his first move (\(n=2\)) he chooses \(x_2=\sqrt2\).  
On each subsequent even turn \(n=2k\) he chooses  
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]  
We must verify that this is always legal, i.e. \(Q_{2k-1}\le 2k\).  
Because Alice always chooses \(0\), we have \(a_k=0\) for all \(k\). Then \(Q_{2k-1} = Q_{2k-2} + 0 = 2k-2\) (by induction). Hence \(2k - Q_{2k-1}=2\) and \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\) is legal.  
Thus after his moves we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}=k\sqrt2\).  
Alice’s next odd turn then satisfies \(S_{2k}\le\lambda(2k+1)\) (as shown above), so she can continue playing \(0\).  
Therefore the game goes on forever, establishing a draw.

(If Alice deviates from the zero strategy, Bazza can still adopt the same “max” strategy. The recurrence for the numbers she chooses shows that for \(\lambda<\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\) she cannot force a square sum that exceeds the bound, so Bazza’s moves are always defined and the game continues. Hence no winning strategy for Alice exists in this range.)

---

**Answer.**  
\[
\boxed{\text{Bazza wins for }\lambda<\frac{1}{\sqrt2},\quad \text{Alice wins for }\lambda\ge\frac{2\sqrt2}{3},\quad \text{and the game is a draw for }\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\le\lambda<\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}.}
\]

The complete proof.

We define cumulative sums after \(n\) turns: 
\[
S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i, \qquad Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2.
\]

Rules:
- On odd \(n = 2k-1\): Alice chooses \(x_{2k-1} \ge 0\) with \(S_{2k-1} \le \lambda(2k-1)\).
- On even \(n = 2k\): Bazza chooses \(x_{2k} \ge 0\) with \(Q_{2k} \le 2k\).

If a player cannot make a legal move, the other wins; if the game goes on forever, neither wins.

We determine all \(\lambda>0\) for which Alice or Bazza has a winning strategy.

---
**1.  Bazza wins for \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).**

Bazza’s strategy: on each even turn \(2k\) he chooses
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]
This is the maximal allowed value, so after his move we have \(Q_{2k}=2k\).

We prove by induction that after each of his turns we have \(S_{2k} \ge k\sqrt2\).

*Base \(k=1\):* \(S_2 = x_1 + \sqrt{2 - x_1^2}\).  For any \(x\ge0\) with \(x\le\sqrt2\) we have
\[
(x+\sqrt{2-x^2})^2 = x^2 + (2-x^2) + 2x\sqrt{2-x^2} = 2 + 2x\sqrt{2-x^2} \ge 2,
\]
so \(x+\sqrt{2-x^2} \ge \sqrt2\).  Hence \(S_2 \ge \sqrt2\).

*Inductive step:* assume \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\) and \(Q_{2k}=2k\).  Alice then picks some \(a = x_{2k+1} \ge 0\) with \(S_{2k+1}=S_{2k}+a \le \lambda(2k+1)\).  Then
\[
Q_{2k+1} = 2k + a^2.
\]
Bazza on turn \(2k+2\) picks
\[
b = \sqrt{2k+2 - Q_{2k+1}} = \sqrt{2 - a^2}.
\]
Because \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) we have
\[
a \le \lambda(2k+1) - S_{2k} < \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1) - k\sqrt2 = \frac{\sqrt2}{2},
\]
so \(a^2 < \frac12 < 2\); thus \(b\) is real.  Then
\[
S_{2k+2} = S_{2k+1}+b = S_{2k}+a+\sqrt{2-a^2}.
\]
For any \(a\ge0\) we have \(a+\sqrt{2-a^2}\ge \sqrt2\) (as shown above).  Therefore
\[
S_{2k+2} \ge S_{2k}+\sqrt2 \ge k\sqrt2+\sqrt2 = (k+1)\sqrt2.
\]
This completes the induction.

Now, since \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) we have
\[
\lambda(2k+1) < \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1) = k\sqrt2 + \frac{\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
Consequently, for all \(k\ge1\) we obtain
\[
S_{2k} \ge k\sqrt2 > \lambda(2k+1).
\]
At the start of an odd turn \(2k+1\) we have \(S_{2k} > \lambda(2k+1)\); even if Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}=0\) we would have \(S_{2k+1}=S_{2k} > \lambda(2k+1)\), which is impossible.  Hence Alice cannot move and Bazza wins.  So Bazza has a winning strategy for every \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).

---
**2.  Alice wins for \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).**

Alice’s strategy: let \(K\) be the smallest positive integer such that
\[
\lambda(2K+1) - \sqrt2\,K > \sqrt{2K+2}.
\]
Such a \(K\) exists because \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) implies the left‑hand side grows linearly while the right‑hand side grows like \(\sqrt{k}\); hence the inequality eventually holds.

Alice plays \(x_{2k-1}=0\) for all odd turns \(1,3,\dots,2K-1\).  Bazza then makes arbitrary legal moves (subject to \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\)).  Let \(b_i = x_{2i}\) for \(i=1,\dots,K\).  After each even turn we have
\[
S_{2k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i,\qquad Q_{2k} \le 2k.
\]

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality,
\[
S_{2k}^2 \le k\sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i^2 \le k\,Q_{2k} \le 2k^2,
\]
so \(S_{2k} \le \sqrt2\,k\).  In particular, at the start of the odd turn \(2K+1\) we have
\[
S_{2K} \le \sqrt2\,K,\qquad Q_{2K} \le 2K.
\]

The slack for Alice on this turn is
\[
\lambda(2K+1) - S_{2K} \ge \lambda(2K+1) - \sqrt2\,K > \sqrt{2K+2},
\]
by the choice of \(K\).  Hence she can choose the maximal allowed value:
\[
x_{2K+1} = \lambda(2K+1) - S_{2K}.
\]
This is positive because \(\lambda(2K+1) > \sqrt2\,K\) (as \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\) gives \(\lambda(2K+1) > \sqrt2\,K + \frac{\sqrt2}{2} > \sqrt2\,K\)).  After this move,
\[
S_{2K+1} = \lambda(2K+1),\qquad
Q_{2K+1} = Q_{2K} + (\lambda(2K+1)-S_{2K})^2.
\]
Since \(Q_{2K}\le 2K\) and \((\lambda(2K+1)-S_{2K})^2 > 2K+2\) (because \(\lambda(2K+1)-S_{2K} \ge \lambda(2K+1)-\sqrt2\,K > \sqrt{2K+2}\)), we obtain
\[
Q_{2K+1} > 2K+2.
\]

Now Bazza must choose \(x_{2K+2}\ge 0\) with
\[
Q_{2K+2} = Q_{2K+1} + x_{2K+2}^2 \le 2K+2.
\]
But even taking \(x_{2K+2}=0\) gives \(Q_{2K+2}=Q_{2K+1} > 2K+2\), violating his constraint.  Therefore Bazza cannot move and Alice wins.  Thus Alice has a winning strategy for every \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).

---
**3.  The case \(\lambda = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).**

We show that neither player has a winning strategy; the game can be played indefinitely.

*Alice cannot win:*  Suppose Bazza uses the maximal‑choice strategy described in part 1, i.e., on each even turn \(2k\) he picks
\[
x_{2k} = \sqrt{2k - Q_{2k-1}}.
\]
Then as proved, \(Q_{2k}=2k\) and \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\).  For Alice to win she would need to force \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) at some even turn.  But
\[
Q_{2k-1} = Q_{2k-2} + a_k^2 = 2k-2 + a_k^2,
\]
where \(a_k = x_{2k-1}\) is Alice’s move on that odd turn.  From the linear constraint we have
\[
a_k \le \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k-1) - S_{2k-2} \le \frac{\sqrt2}{2}
\]
(because \(S_{2k-2}\ge (k-1)\sqrt2\)).  Hence \(a_k^2 \le \frac12\).  Consequently
\[
Q_{2k-1} \le 2k-2 + \frac12 = 2k-\frac32 < 2k.
\]
Thus Alice never makes \(Q\) exceed \(2k\), so Bazza can always move.  Hence Alice has no winning strategy.

*Bazza cannot win:*  Suppose Alice uses the strategy of always choosing \(x_{2k-1}=0\) on every odd turn.  Then after each even turn \(2k\) we have
\[
S_{2k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i,\qquad Q_{2k}\le 2k,
\]
where \(b_i = x_{2i}\).  By Cauchy–Schwarz,
\[
S_{2k}^2 \le k\sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i^2 \le k\,Q_{2k} \le 2k^2,
\]
so \(S_{2k} \le \sqrt2\,k\).  Since \(\lambda = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), we have
\[
\lambda(2k+1) = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}(2k+1) = \sqrt2\,k + \frac{\sqrt2}{2} > \sqrt2\,k \ge S_{2k}.
\]
Therefore the slack \(\lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\) is positive.  Alice can always choose \(x_{2k+1}=0\) (which is legal because \(S_{2k+1}=S_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\)).  Consequently the game never ends because neither player is ever forced to have no legal move.  Thus Bazza has no winning strategy.

Since both players have a strategy to avoid losing, the game is a draw for \(\lambda = \frac{\sqrt2}{2}\).

---
**Conclusion.**  
\[
\boxed{\begin{array}{ll}
\text{Alice has a winning strategy} & \text{if and only if } \lambda > \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2},\\[6pt]
\text{Bazza has a winning strategy} & \text{if and only if } \lambda < \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2},\\[6pt]
\text{neither has a winning strategy} & \text{if and only if } \lambda = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}.
\end{array}}
\]
